content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY                                GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
SOUTH COAST AREA
245 WEST BROADWAY, SUITE 380
LONG BEACH, CA  90802
(213) 590-5071


January 16, 1990


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807
1100 K Street Building
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE:  Request for Formal Opinion of Potential Conflict of Interest

I am currently employed by the California Coastal Commission as a supervising
staff analyst in the Long Beach office. I am contemplating a particular type
of work involving consulting on a "project" which I have been directly
involved during the past two years.

The project is called the Orange County Affordable Housing program, for which
the Coastal Commission has been responsible for administering the past two
years.  The program involves some several hundred homeowners who are under the
program's resale control provisions.  All of the currently restricted units
resulted from previous permits being approved by the Commission with the
requirement to provide a percentage of affordable units with resale control
provisions.

Although the requirement for affordable housing was removed from the Coastal
Act in 1981, those permits mentioned above were approved prior to the
amendment to the Coastal Act.  I began working for the Commission in June
1984, and did not therefore work on those initial permits.

During 1988, about 30-40 or so homeowners submitted amendments to those
original permits pertaining to their respective projects, requesting that the
Commission delete the resale controls and allow them to sell at market
prices.  The Commission denied their requests, and instead, imposed special
conditions which allowed them to sell under certain conditions.  It is now the
procedure to allow individuals to submit amendment requests to enable them to
sell under these new conditions.

My current duties involve administering the program to the extent of answering
general inquiries as well as handling individual amendment request proposals.
An amendment request may or may not result in a unit being sold, but
nevertheless allows the unit to be sold consistent with the Commission's
special conditions.  The ultimate sale of a unit must be reviewed and approved
by the Commission before escrow can close. I typically perform that review
and approval function.

Questions:

1) Assuming that all of the amendments for which I have prepared for
Commission approval are a "proceeding" which I was directly involved, may
future amendment requests and sales not be considered the same proceeding
should I act in the capacity to consult for these requestors?

Fair Political Practices Commission
Page 2

2) If I were to consult for a non-profit entity which was administering the
resale program, would there be any conflict of interests if I were performing
certain tasks outlined in the attached "RFP" (Request for Proposal). In what
capacity would my tasks and or activities constitute a conflict?

3) Would there be a conflict if I were to consult for homeowners after the
"agency" awarded the contract begins administering the program?

Attached are some background documents which you may find helpful. If there
are any questions, please call me at (213) 590-5071. Please send the response
letter to my home address please:

John Leslie
14 La Cueva
Rancho Santa Margarita CA 92688

Thank you for your prompt reply.

Sincerely,

[signature]

John Leslie

0158V

STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY                                    GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
SOUTH COAST AREA
245 WEST BROADWAY, SUITE 380
LONG BEACH, CA  90802
(213) 590-5071


January 16, 1990


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807
1100 K Street Building
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE:  Request for Formal Opinion of Potential Conflict of Interest

I am currently employed by the California Coastal Commission as a supervising
staff analyst in the Long Beach office. I am contemplating a particular type
of work involving consulting on a "project" which I have been directly
involved during the past two years.

The project is called the Orange County Affordable Housing program, for which
the Coastal Commission has been responsible for administering the past two

Although the requirement for affordable housing was removed from the Coastal

amendment to the Coastal Act.  I began working for the Commission in June
1984, and did not therefore work on those initial permits.

During 1988, about 30-40 or so homeowners submitted amendments to those
original permits pertaining to their respective projects, requesting that the
Commission delete the resale controls and allow them to sell at market

conditions which allowed them to sell under certain conditions.  It is now the
procedure to allow individuals to submit amendment requests to enable them to
sell under these new conditions.

My current duties involve administering the program to the extent of answering
general inquiries as well as handling individual amendment request proposals.
An amendment request may or may not result in a unit being sold, but
nevertheless allows the unit to be sold consistent with the Commission's
special conditions.  The ultimate sale of a unit must be reviewed and approved
by the Commission before escrow can close.  I typically perform that review

Commission approval are a "proceeding" which I was directly involved, may
future amendment requests and sales not be considered the same proceeding
should I act in the capacity to consult for these requestors?

Fair Political Practices Commission
Page 2

2) If I were to consult for a non-profit entity which was administering the
resale program, would there be any conflict of interest if I were performing
certain tasks outlined in the attached "RFP" (Request for Proposal). In what
capacity would my tasks and or activities constitute a conflict?

3) Would there be a conflict if I were to consult for homeowners after the
"agency" awarded the contract begins administering the program?

Attached are some background documents which you may find helpful. If there
are any questions, please call me at (213) 590-5071. Please send the response
letter to my home address please:

John Leslie
14 La Cueva
Rancho Santa Margarita CA 92688

Thank you for your prompt reply.

Sincerely,

[signature]
John Leslie

0158V

RECEIVED
DEC 07 1989
CALIFORNIA
COASTAL COMMISSION

CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
REQUEST FOR PROPOSALS

The Coastal Commission is seeking eligible nonprofit entities for
disbursement of a $150,000 grant for the purposes of demonstrat-
ing the feasibility of administering the Affordable Housing Pro-
gram in the coastal zone of Orange County. (See map at
attachment A).

Project History

1977-1982
From 1977 through 1982, the California Coastal Act contained
specific policies requiring the provision of affordable housing
within the Coastal Zone. Under that authority, the Commission
required as a condition of development the dedication of units
for low and moderate income housing. In the unincorporated areas
of the Orange County coastal zone almost 600 affordable housing
units were developed. To ensure the continued affordability of
the units, the Commission imposed a condition that the original
applicants establish programs to regulate future sales of the
units.

As a result, the applicants set up a resale program to be admin-
istered by the Orange County Housing Authority. The Authority
had the option to purchase the units at controlled prices and, in
turn, the Authority could assign that option to eligible purchas-
ers. If the option was not exercised, the purchasers could then
sell at market rates. Certain units contained a specific provis-
ion, termed a recapture provision, that turned over the differ-
ence between the controlled price and the market price to the
Coastal Commission.

1983-1988
In August 1983, the Orange County Housing Authority gave notice
of its intent to withdraw from the program. The Authority was
succeeded by Community Housing Enterprise (CHE). CHE terminated
its participation in 1987 citing (1) a lack of legal resources,
(2) harassment by hostile homeowners, (3) lack of low-interest
financing and (4) inability to retain adequate experienced staff.

At the time of CHE's withdrawal, there were slightly over 500
units in the program. The number of units in the program had
declined as sales were made at market rates owing to the owner's
inability or unwillingness to sell to a qualified buyer. It is
possible that a number of units were sold illegally, without the
knowledge of any administering agency.

-2-

1988-1989
Since the CHE's withdrawal, the program has been without an
administrator. The Coastal Commission originally considered
releasing some owners from the resale requirements citing hard-
ships. However, on advice from the Attorney General, the Commission has allowed sale of restricted units only if the funds
representing the difference between the selling price and the
deed restricted price are deposited into an escrow account under
the jurisdiction of the Commission (i.e., "recaptured"). Regardless of the selling price, a minimum of $10.00 in recaptured
funds must be realized by the Commission for each unit that is
released. The Attorney General's ruling determined that sale of
the units without any recapture provisions would be a "gift of
public funds", which is constitutionally prohibited.

Although the Commission has a mechanism to eliminate the gift of
public funds, the program was slowly being liquidated. In order
to initiate a program to ensure continuing administration numerous and complex questions had to be answered. Basic data regarding the program was lacking. The data was considered necessary
to fine tune the program and develop a management plan that will
ensure furtherance of its original intent. Gathering this essential information necessitated a physical presence in Orange County and certain types of skills and housing expertise that is
unavailable from Coastal Commission or Legislative staff. For
these reasons it was necessary to contract for a study to obtain
this data before proceeding further in developing a management
plan.

Study by the Fair Housing Council of Orange County

In an effort to fashion a solution to the problem of future
administration of the Affordable Housing Program, the Coastal
Commission made a grant to the Fair Housing Council of Orange
County. The grant was issued with the support of Senator David
Roberti. During the study, Senator Roberti's office provided
technical assistance to the Coastal Commission.

The study, "Survey of Orange County Coastal Housing", was completed in December of 1989. The study focused on:

•    Conducting a full inventory of the remaining units
     within the program.

•    Analyzing the operation of the program to determine if
     it continues to remain feasible.

•    Proposing alternative models for future administration.

A copy of the study is enclosed as attachment B.

-3-

Results of the Study. The study identified 416 units that are
still subject to resale controls. These are located in seven
different developments. Control over reselling of these units is
exercised through an Attachment to Grant Deed. The resale
controls provisions are incorporated into the Attachment to Grant
Deed which is then recorded with the deed at the time the unit is
sold.

There is a basic procedure for conducting a sale of a controlled
unit. The seller must notify the administering agency of their
intent to sell. The administering agency is then given a period
of time within which to either find a qualified buyer and assign
the benefits of the purchase option to the buyer, or to buy the
property itself at the controlled price. The Attachment to Grant
Deed contains a formula for calculating the controlled price.
Generally, when the unit is sold the administering agency can
collect a 3% resale fee.

The study found that in most cases the market value of the units
exceeded the controlled price, hence the units still were capable
of providing affordable housing opportunities. Most of the units
are not affordable to eligible buyers through conventional
financing. However, the units are affordable when low-interest
loans and first-time home buyer programs such as those offered by
the California Housing Finance Agency are used.

The report recommended that an administrative agency be selected
to operate the program. The administrative agency should have an
on-site presence and have the capability to make aggressive
efforts to market the units when they were made available for
resale.

Information for Prospective Bidders

All parties interested in applying for this grant must respond
with a proposal by 5:00 DATE. The proposal must be sent to:

California Coastal Commission
631 Howard St., Fourth Floor
San Francisco, CA 94105
Attention: __________

The proposal must address the recommended program developed in
the study "Survey of Orange County Coastal Zone Housing", by the
Fair Housing Council of Orange County.

If the proposal chooses to adopt all or portions of the general
enforcement programs described in that study the actual implemen-
tation plan should be described in detail. Whether or not the
proposal adopts all or part of the program outlined in that study

-4-

the proposal must specifically include a detailed description of
the following:

1. A proposal for establishing contact with the owners and occu-
pants of the inclusionary units. This proposal should include:

• A means for verifying the current occupancy of the unit
and determining whether there has been any violation of
the terms of the Attachment to Grant Deed.

• An ongoing campaign of contact and education to acquaint
current owners and occupants with the terms of the
Attachment to Grant Deed.

2. A detailed marketing plan which will result in:

• A pool of eligible prequalified buyers prepared to pur-
chase within the time limits of the Attachment to Grant
Deed.

• A financing package that meets the needs of those poten-
tial buyers.

• Availability of the units to all segments of the Orange
County housing market, particularly minorities and
female-headed households.

3. A detailed Attachment to Grant Deed enforcement plan that
permits all inclusionary units to be purchased by eligible buyers
despite aggressive enforcement of the Attachment to Grant Deed
terms.

4. A detailed budget [illegible] sources of revenue an programs,need
ad resale and resale fees

5. All proposals should also address the following:

• Provide recommendations that will minimize the possibil-
ity of unauthorized sales.

• List the staff resources that will be needed to adminis-
ter the program including any special assistance that
will be expected from the Coastal Commission.

• List and describe the actions that will be taken to deal
with uncooperative sellers [illegible] and seller

• It may be necessary to purchase units should eligible
buyers not be found. The proposal should describe how
an emergency fund will be built up and the desirable
size of the fund.

-5-

• A new Attachment to Grant Deed must be developed which
includes a recapture clause. Describe the faults with
the existing Attachments to Grant Deed and provide rec-
ommendations for improvements.

In order to adequately respond to these points, prospective bid-
ders may want to review the volumes of background material that
the Fair Housing Council collected while undertaking their study.
This material is available for review in the Long Beach office of
the California Coastal Commission located at 245 West Broadway,
Suite 380, Long Beach. The office number is (213) 590-5071.
These volumes are entitled:

• Inventory Management Tools

• Marketing Management Tools

• Study Exhibits

The proposal must also submit a statement of qualifications which
should include all experience that relates to the objective of
the program, including experience in:

• Securing financing both from conventional and
below-market sources.

• Managing controlled resale programs.

• Developing marketing programs for the sale of residen-
tial properties.

The statement of qualifications should also list the qualifica-
tions and experience of any specialized staff that will be needed
such as a real estate broker, attorney and appraiser and how the
staff will be used in the program.

Administrative Information
This is a demonstration program to manage these units consistent
with the original intent of the program for a limited term. The
original intent of the program was to provide owner-occupied
units for low and moderate income households. The program will
run for a duration of 18 months. After that period there is the
possibility of a contract being signed to continue the program
permanently.

The selected grantee will sign the contract attached as exhibit
C.

-6-

The payment schedule will be as follows:

(To be completed later).

Evaluation of Proposals
Proposals will be evaluated based on how closely the proposals
meet the requirements stated in this Request for Proposals. The
amount of the grant will be $150,000.

Coastal Commission Role
The Coastal Commission will maintain a role as the monitoring
agency. The Commission will audit administrative agency service,
records and related finances. The Commission will also regularly
review the performance on the administrative agency. It is not
the intention of the Commission to have a day-to-day role in
administering the program.

-7 -

List of Attachments

A.  Map of area showing affordable housing units.

B.  Copy of study done by the Fair Housing Council of Orange
    County under a grant from Coastal Commission, entitled, "Sur-
    vey of Orange County Coastal Zone Housing."

C.  Copy of contract that will be used by the Coastal Commission.
    Their staff will need to draft the contract.

STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY                    GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
SOUTH COAST AREA
245 WEST BROADWAY, SUITE 380
LONG BEACH, CA  90802
(213) 590-5071

ORANGE COUNTY AFFORDABLE HOUSING
STATUS REPORT AND PROCEDURES


TO: Interested Persons
FROM: Chuck Damm, South Coast District Director
DATE: October 12, 1988


The purpose of this memo is to clarify some of the issues and questions
regarding the Coastal Commission's obligation to carry out a resale program
for affordable housing in Orange County. Because of the County's
unwillingness to administer the program and the absence of any other
administering agency, the Coastal Commission has become ultimately
responsible for the continuation of the resale program. The following
describes how the current situation has evolved and the current procedures
for compliance with resale restrictions:

Background

From 1977 to 1982 Section 30213 of the Coastal Act contained a specific
policy requiring the provision of housing opportunities for persons of low
and moderate income within the coastal zone. Under that authority the
Commission required, as conditions to coastal development permits for
residential development, the inclusion of affordable housing units with
resale controls.

In order to assure the continued affordability of these units, the
conditions imposed by the Commission also required applicants to establish
or participate in programs to regulate the subsequent sales of the units.
In order to set up a program to regulate the sales of these units, the
Commission agreed to allow the Orange County Housing Authority (OCHA) to
administer the resale control program. Under an agreement, OCHA was granted
the right to purchase the units at controlled prices and to assign the
units to eligible buyers when the units are offered for sale by the
owners. The agreement provides that the units could be sold by the owners
at market rate, and would no longer be affordable, if the units were not
purchased at controlled prices within a certain time frame.

In August, 1983, the Orange County Housing Authority notified the
Commission of its withdrawal, effective February, 1984, as implementing
agency for the Program pursuant to a policy adopted by the Orange County
Board of Supervisors to terminate existing resale control agreements
administered by OCHA. The Orange County Housing Authority then transferred
the administration of the program to Community Housing Enterprises (CHE), a
non-profit organization. The program was administered by CHE until August
31, 1987, at which time CHE withdrew from the Program.

Page 2

CHE's decision to withdraw resulted in owners of affordable units
requesting information as to how their units could be sold given that CHE
was no longer available to administer the program. The staff of the
Commission advised owners to apply for an amendment to the coastal permit,
which originally established the resale control program, in order to be
released from its terms. Numerous amendment requests to delete the resale
controls were eventually scheduled for public hearings in July and
September 1988.

After the staff had encouraged owners to apply for permit amendments but
before the Commission had acted, the Attorney General's Office issued a
legal opinion which concluded that the release of homeowners from the
resale controls constitutes a gift of public funds and violates the
California Constitution. Accordingly, the staff changed its recommendation
to the Commission to one of denial of the amendment requests as submitted
(complete release from resale controls) and approval with certain
conditions.

At its July and September 1988, meetings the Commission voted not to
eliminate the resale controls on the applicant's units. Instead, the
Commission granted these amendments subject to several conditions (see
attachment 1). The Commission's actions apply only to those units owned by
persons who had applications before the Commission. In addition, the
Commission's action is not binding on any applicant unless and until the
permit amendment conditions are accepted by the applicant.

The Commission directed the staff to inform homeowners wanting to sell
their units, that these homeowners may pursue either one of the
following options:

1) Amendment to Delete the Resale Controls.

A homeowner may apply for an amendment to delete the resale provisions in
the Attachment to Grant Deed which will enable the owner to sell the unit
provided the homeowner incorporates the Commission's special conditions in
their amendment request (attachment 1). In accepting the special
conditions, the homeowner's amendment request may be expedited and approved
as an amendment without a regular public hearing. A homeowner may disagree
with the conditions and may request the Commission to hold a full hearing
to make an individual determination whether the conditions are
appropriate. Applicants should be aware that the Commission has already
had a full hearing on the matter and approved numerous amendments subject
to conditions as outlined in attachment 1. It is unlikely the Commission
will deal with future amendment requests in a different manner unless the
applicant can clearly show unique circumstances that warrant a new decision.

2) Sell the Unit Under the Existing Program.

Homeowners wishing to sell their unit under the existing program may do so
provided the homeowner locate an eligible buyer. To sell your unit, you
must submit the following paperwork to the Commission staff:

1) a Notice of Intent to Sell and Property Information form and
2) a copy of the Grant Deed and Attachment from the seller.

Page 3

In order for the Commission staff to approve a buyer as being eligible
under the program, the following information about the potential buyer is
needed:

1) a current pay stub,
2) a statement of family composition and income and
3) a copy of the previous year's tax return.

Upon receipt of these materials the staff will then determine if the
potential buyer is qualified, provide you with the restricted resale price,
and provide the escrow company with a signed and notarized Notice of
Acceptance form. This process is relatively simple and can be handled at
the staff level. Commission staff receives periodic calls from prospective
qualified buyers seeking affordable units for sale under the program.

Attorney General's Opinion. Because the Attorney General's office has
informed the Commission that simply releasing homeowners from the resale
controls constitutes an illegal gift of public funds, homeowners submitting
a Notice of Intent to Sell form and expecting a release after the time has
expired will not be processed without conforming to one of the options
stated above.

Amendment request forms (for option #1) are available by calling
(213) 590-5071. Ask for an Affordable Housing Amendment Form. Homeowners
wanting to sell under option #2 or wishing to discuss option #1 in greater
detail, should contact [illegible] at (213) 590-5071.

I hope this information is helpful to you.

7527A

ATTACHMENT 1 - SPECIAL CONDITIONS

1) In the event a public agency or a non-profit organization acceptable to
the Commission agrees to assume responsibility for the administration of the
resale control procedures in effect at the time this application was filed,
such resale control procedures shall be automatically reinstated upon
Commission approval of the assignment of administration responsibility and the
approval of this amendment shall be null and void.

2) In lieu of compliance with the resale control provisions in effect at the
time this application was filed, the applicant may proceed with the sale of
the affected residential unit upon the applicant entering into an agreement in
the form of an addendum to escrow instructions (Exhibit 2) which provides for
the recapture of that portion of the sales price which exceeds the net amount
the applicant would have received under the resale price formula set forth in
the applicable resale control procedures. All portions of the sale price
subject to recapture shall be paid to the Commission or another public agency
designated by the Commission for deposit in a special escrow account and shall
be used solely for the purpose of protecting or providing affordable housing
opportunities in Orange County. The sale of any unit under this amendment
shall be subject to the concurrence of the Executive Director who may withhold
such concurrence if he determines that the unit is being sold below Fair
Market Value.

3) The recapture provisions of special condition 2 shall be applicable to
subsequent purchasers until either (a) the amount of surplus funds associated
with a single sale and subject to recapture exceeds $10,000, or (b) the term
of the original resale control restrictions expires. All surplus funds
generated by each individual sale of the residential unit shall be subject to
recapture and deposit in the escrow account specified in special condition 2
notwithstanding the fact that the aggregate of such funds resulting from multiple
sales of the same unit may exceed $10,000.

1) Assuming that all of the amendments for which I have prepared for Commission approval are a "proceeding" which I was directly involved, may future amendment requests and sales not be considered the same proceeding should I act in the capacity to consult for these requestors?

Fair Political Practices Commission

2) If I were to consult for a non-profit entity which was administering the resale program, would there be any conflict of interests if I were performing certain tasks outlined in the attached "RFP" (Request for Proposal). In what capacity would my tasks and or activities constitute a conflict?

3) Would there be a conflict if I were to consult for homeowners after the "agency" awarded the contract begins administering the program?

Attached are some background documents which you may find helpful. If there are any questions, please call me at (213) 590-5071. Please send the response letter to my home address please:

John Leslie
14 La Cueva
Rancho Santa Margarita CA 92688

Thank you for your prompt reply.
sections.facts:
From 1977 to 1982 Section 30213 of the Coastal Act contained a specific policy requiring the provision of housing opportunities for persons of low and moderate income within the coastal zone. Under that authority the Commission required, as conditions to coastal development permits for residential development, the inclusion of affordable housing units with resale controls.

In order to assure the continued affordability of these units, the conditions imposed by the Commission also required applicants to establish or participate in programs to regulate the subsequent sales of the units. In order to set up a program to regulate the sales of these units, the Commission agreed to allow the Orange County Housing Authority (OCHA) to administer the resale control program. Under an agreement, OCHA was granted the right to purchase the units at controlled prices and to assign the units to eligible buyers when the units are offered for sale by the owners. The agreement provides that the units could be sold by the owners at market rate, and would no longer be affordable, if the units were not purchased at controlled prices within a certain time frame.

In August, 1983, the Orange County Housing Authority notified the Commission of its withdrawal, effective February, 1984, as implementing agency for the Program pursuant to a policy adopted by the Orange County Board of Supervisors to terminate existing resale control agreements administered by OCHA. The Orange County Housing Authority then transferred the administration of the program to Community Housing Enterprises (CHE), a non-profit organization. The program was administered by CHE until August 31, 1987, at which time CHE withdrew from the Program.

CHE's decision to withdraw resulted in owners of affordable units requesting information as to how their units could be sold given that CHE was no longer available to administer the program. The staff of the Commission advised owners to apply for an amendment to the coastal permit, which originally established the resale control program, in order to be released from its terms. Numerous amendment requests to delete the resale controls were eventually scheduled for public hearings in July and September 1988.

After the staff had encouraged owners to apply for permit amendments but before the Commission had acted, the Attorney General's Office issued a legal opinion which concluded that the release of homeowners from the resale controls constitutes a gift of public funds and violates the California Constitution. Accordingly, the staff changed its recommendation to the Commission to one of denial of the amendment requests as submitted (complete release from resale controls) and approval with certain conditions.

At its July and September 1988 meetings the Commission voted not to eliminate the resale controls on the applicant's units. Instead, the Commission granted these amendments subject to several conditions (see attachment 1). The Commission's actions apply only to those units owned by persons who had applications before the Commission. In addition, the Commission's action is not binding on any applicant unless and until the permit amendment conditions are accepted by the applicant.

The Commission directed the staff to inform homeowners wanting to sell their unit, that these homeowners may pursue either one of the following options:

1) Amendment to Delete the Resale Controls.

A homeowner may apply for an amendment to delete the resale provisions in the Attachment to Grant Deed which will enable the owner to sell the unit provided the homeowner incorporates the Commission's special conditions in their amendment request (attachment 1). In accepting the special conditions, the homeowner's amendment request may be expedited and approved as an amendment without a regular public hearing. A homeowner may disagree with the conditions and may request the Commission to hold a full hearing to make an individual determination whether the conditions are appropriate. Applicants should be aware that the Commission has already had a full hearing on the matter and approved numerous amendments subject to conditions as outlined in attachment 1. It is unlikely the Commission will deal with future amendment requests in a different manner unless the applicant can clearly show unique circumstances that warrant a new decision.

2) Sell the Unit Under the Existing Program.

Homeowners wishing to sell their unit under the existing program may do so provided the homeowner locate an eligible buyer. To sell your unit, you must submit the following paperwork to the Commission staff:

1) a Notice of Intent to Sell and Property Information form and
2) a copy of the Grant Deed and Attachment from the seller.

In order for the Commission staff to approve a buyer as being eligible under the program, the following information about the potential buyer is needed:

1) a current pay stub,
2) a statement of family composition and income and
3) a copy of the previous year's tax return.

Upon receipt of these materials the staff will then determine if the potential buyer is qualified, provide you with the restricted resale price, and provide the escrow company with a signed and notarized Notice of Acceptance form. This process is relatively simple and can be handled at the staff level. Commission staff receives periodic calls from prospective qualified buyers seeking affordable units for sale under the program.

Attorney General's Opinion. Because the Attorney General's office has informed the Commission that simply releasing homeowners from the resale controls constitutes an illegal gift of public funds, homeowners submitting a Notice of Intent to Sell form and expecting a release after the time has expired will not be processed without conforming to one of the options stated above.

Amendment request forms (for option #1) are available by calling (213)590-5071. Ask for an Affordable Housing Amendment Form. Homeowners wanting to sell under option #2 or wishing to discuss option #1 in greater detail, should contact Michael Pauls at (213) 590-5071.

I hope this information is helpful to you.

7527A

ATTACHMENT 1 - SPECIAL CONDITIONS

1) In the event a public agency or a non-profit organization acceptable to the Commission agrees to assume responsibility for the administration of the resale control procedures in effect at the time this application was filed such resale control procedures shall be automatically reinstated upon Commission approval of the assignment of administration responsibility and the approval of this amendment shall be null and void.

2) In lieu of compliance with the resale control provisions in effect at the time this application was filed, the applicant may proceed with the sale of the affected residential unit upon the applicant entering into an agreement in the form of an addendum to escrow instructions (Exhibit 21) which provides for the recapture of that portion of the sales price which exceeds the net amount the applicant would have received under the resale price formula set forth in the applicable resale control procedures. All portions of the sale price subject to recapture shall be paid to the Commission or another public agency designated by the Commission for deposit in a special escrow account and shall be used solely for the purpose of protecting or providing affordable housing opportunities in Orange County. The sale of any unit under this amendment shall be subject to the concurrence of the Executive Director who may withhold such concurrence if he determines that the unit is being sold below Fair Market Value.

3) The recapture provisions of special condition 2 shall be applicable to subsequent purchasers until either (a) the amount of surplus funds associated with a single sale and subject to recapture exceeds $10,000, or (b) the term of the original resale control restrictions expires. All surplus funds generated by each individual sale of the residential unit shall be subject to recapture and deposit in the escrow account specified in special condition 2 notwithstanding the fact the aggregate of such funds resulting from multiple sales of the same unit may exceed $10,000.

embedding.first_500_words: January 16, 1990 Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 1100 K Street Building Sacramento, CA 95814 RE: Request for Formal Opinion of Potential Conflict of Interest I am currently employed by the California Coastal Commission as a supervising staff analyst in the Long Beach office. I am contemplating a particular type of work involving consulting on a "project" which I have been directly involved during the past two years. The project is called the Orange County Affordable Housing program, for which the Coastal Commission has been responsible for administering the past two years. The program involves some several hundred homeowners who are under the program's resale control provisions. All of the currently restricted units resulted from previous permits being approved by the Commission with the requirement to provide a percentage of affordable units with resale control provisions. Although the requirement for affordable housing was removed from the Coastal Act in 1981, those permits mentioned above were approved prior to the amendment to the Coastal Act. I began working for the Commission in June 1984, and did not therefore work on those initial permits. During 1988, about 30-40 or so homeowners submitted amendments to those original permits pertaining to their respective projects, requesting that the Commission delete the resale controls and allow them to sell at market prices. The Commission denied their requests, and instead, imposed special conditions which allowed them to sell under certain conditions. It is now the procedure to allow individuals to submit amendment requests to enable them to sell under these new conditions. My current duties involve administering the program to the extent of answering general inquiries as well as handling individual amendment request proposals. An amendment request may or may not result in a unit being sold, but nevertheless allows the unit to be sold consistent with the Commission's special conditions. The ultimate sale of a unit must be reviewed and approved by the Commission before escrow can close. I typically perform that review and approval function. Questions: 1) Assuming that all of the amendments for which I have prepared for Commission approval are a "proceeding" which I was directly involved, may future amendment requests and sales not be considered the same proceeding should I act in the capacity to consult for these requestors? Fair Political Practices Commission 2) If I were to consult for a non-profit entity which was administering the resale program, would there be any conflict of interests if I were performing certain tasks outlined in the attached "RFP" (Request for Proposal). In what capacity would my tasks and or activities constitute a conflict? 3) Would there be a conflict if I were to consult for homeowners after the "agency" awarded the contract begins administering the program? Attached are some background documents which you may find helpful. If there are any questions, please call me at (213) 590-5071.
